claim_under_attack: Netflix can capture most of the strategic benefits of separation through internal P&L and operating decoupling without imposing a second brand, site, billing flow, and queue system on customers.
evidence_or_reason: This claim is asserted without any supporting evidence that internal-only separation actually delivers comparable strategic benefits to a full split. The artifact offers no case studies, analogues, or data showing that companies with internally separated but consumer-unified declining-and-growing business pairs successfully avoid the organizational drag that motivated the split in the first place. The case packet states that 'product decisions for streaming are constrained by DVD logistics' — Side A never quantifies how much of that constraint is org-chart versus customer-facing, and never explains the mechanism by which internal P&L separation resolves product-level coupling (shared engineering teams, shared recommendation algorithms, shared UI real estate, shared customer support queues). The claim that you get 'most of the benefit' is a bare assertion doing heavy structural work in the argument — it is the linchpin that makes reversal costless on the strategic dimension — yet it has zero evidentiary support. Side A's own open questions acknowledge they do not know 'how much internal decision-making benefit would Netflix actually gain,' which directly undercuts the confidence with which they assert this claim as a basis for action.
